---
title: "MoECOT Runtime and Multi-Core Orchestration"
chapter_id: "moecot-runtime-and-multi-core-orchestration"
part_id: "routing-compression-representation-substrates"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-25"
primary_sources:
  - "octopus_router"
  - "rmi"
  - "benchmaxxing"
  - "beastbrain"
  - "viea"
  - "scf"
  - "talos"
  - "moecot_md"
  - "moecot"
  - "project_theseus_whitepaper"
  - "theseus_operator_os"
  - "theseus_architecture_gate"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Appendix C now records exact source-note mappings for the core claim; support remains at argument until MoECOT runtime artifacts, replay records, benchmark records, or reproduced local runs exist."
  - "Only protocol schema/fixture validation is implemented; no MoECOT runtime artifact, replay, or benchmark run has been imported or reproduced for this chapter."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `moecot-runtime-and-multi-core-orchestration` |
| Part | Part III - Routing, Compression, Representation, and Substrates |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-25 |
| Primary source records | `octopus_router`, `rmi`, `benchmaxxing`, `beastbrain`, `viea`, `scf`, `talos`, `moecot_md`, `moecot`, `project_theseus_whitepaper`, `theseus_operator_os`, `theseus_architecture_gate` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `octopus_router`, `rmi`, `benchmaxxing`; supporting: `beastbrain`, `viea`, `scf`, `talos`, `project_theseus_whitepaper`, `theseus_operator_os`, `theseus_architecture_gate`; variant: `moecot_md`; connector/recovery: `moecot` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `octopus_router`, `rmi`, `benchmaxxing`, `beastbrain`, `viea`, `scf`, `talos`, `moecot_md`, `moecot`, `project_theseus_whitepaper`, `theseus_operator_os`, `theseus_architecture_gate`; raw cache: `octopus_router`, `rmi`, `benchmaxxing`, `beastbrain`, `viea`, `scf`, `talos`, `moecot_md`; connector/recovery: `moecot` |
| Test state | MoECOT orchestration fixture validation implemented; `AsiStackProofs.MoECOTRuntime` implements finite runtime-core evidence-reference and unavailable-source promotion gates; runtime replay and benchmark checks remain planned. |

## Drafting guardrail

MoECOT is treated here as implementation-reference context, not as reproduced runtime evidence. Connector access and source notes establish that the whitepaper is readable and relevant. They do not establish local benchmark results, production safety, or current implementation readiness.

## Problem

The book needs at least one concrete runtime shape for the architecture it describes. Otherwise the stack can become a set of persuasive layers without a believable operational surface. A governed ASI stack needs a compact orchestrator, specialist lanes, control-plane gates, ledgers, replay, handoff, and readiness boundaries that line up with the rest of the book.

MoECOT is the current implementation-reference lane for that shape. Its source note describes a governed, skill-native, low-parameter orchestration architecture with a compact orchestrator, specialist lanes, fail-closed control plane, run/task/control-plane ledgers, readiness gates, replay, handoff, and explicit limitations.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

A purely abstract stack can fail to specify runtime state, registry boundaries, readiness gates, replay, and operational artifacts. A named implementation can fail in the opposite direction: branding can stand in for evidence, source-reported benchmarks can be mistaken for reproduced tests, and runtime mechanisms can drift away from the architectural invariants they were meant to instantiate.

This chapter holds the middle position. MoECOT should be mapped to the stack as a crosswalk of runtime records and open evidence gaps. It should not be used as a shortcut around claim discipline.

## Core Claim

[moecot-runtime-and-multi-core-orchestration.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] MoECOT should be treated as the implementation-reference layer for a governed low-parameter multi-core ASI stack once its authenticated source is fully mined.

The claim remains at `argument` support. The MoECOT source note supports implementation-reference discussion of compact orchestration, specialist lanes, control-plane ledgers, readiness gates, replay, and handoff. It does not promote benchmark, safety, or deployment claims without inspected artifacts or reproduced runs.

### Claim-source mapping status

Appendix C now maps this MoECOT runtime claim to all assigned source notes. The mappings support using MoECOT as an implementation-reference crosswalk for compact orchestration, specialist lanes, ledgers, readiness, replay, and handoff, not as reproduced runtime evidence.

| Source | What it supports | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| `octopus_router` | Head/router selection, specialist arms, dynamic loading, permission envelopes, memory routing, local benchmark ratchets, arm cards, split/merge/retire policies, and verification/composition routes. | Does not prove MoECOT or any router implementation; no routing benchmark or specialist runtime run exists here. |
| `rmi` | Head/router, specialist arms, arm registry, memory/permission/tool routers, benchmark ledger, residual escrow ledger, verification/safety layer, regression floors, and lifecycle discipline. | No empirical modular-intelligence result, benchmark reproduction, or prototype inspection has been performed. |
| `benchmaxxing` | Benchmark lifecycle, wall diagnosis, model/benchmark ledgers, anti-Goodhart safeguards, regression conversion, residual preservation, and architecture-change criteria. | No local benchmark harness, mutation, holdout, run, or reproduced performance claim exists here. |
| `beastbrain` | Local/stateful OS-like intelligence process with memory substrate, planning, routing, verification, security, consolidation, interface surfaces, and local deployment vocabulary. | Architecture lineage only; cost, SSD-first, hardware, and benchmark claims are unverified. |
| `viea` | Command contracts, artifacts, specialist-routed work, verified outputs, runtime execution, feedback, residuals, tools, benchmarks, and regression coverage. | No completed VIEA deployment, runtime trace, or verified execution result exists here. |
| `scf` | Runtime cores as replaceable capability implementations behind stable contracts, evidence registries, scoped qualifications, route validation, lifecycle events, incidents, appeals, rollback, and authority boundaries. | Does not prove MoECOT core safety, evaluator quality, or production replacement behavior. |
| `talos` | Typed jobs, deterministic control planes, factory execution, adjudication, delivery, feedback, evidence records, audit logs, replay, tool isolation, and approval gates. | No Talos runtime, MoECOT-Talos integration, replay run, security result, or benchmark has been reproduced. |
| `moecot_md` | Terminology normalization for compact orchestrator, specialist lanes, control-plane ledgers, readiness gates, replay, and fail-closed promotion. | Variant source only; it is not independent corroboration. |
| `moecot` | Compact orchestrator, specialist lanes, fail-closed control plane, run/task/control-plane ledgers, readiness gates, replay, handoff, promotion blockers, residual tracking, and explicit limitations. | Code, logs, benchmark artifacts, replay records, and release artifacts have not been imported, inspected, or reproduced. |
| `project_theseus_whitepaper` | Compact substrate, autonomy control loop, Octopus router, bounded specialist arms, benchmark/residual ledgers, checkpoints, Hive runtime, observability, and trusted-node task allowlists. | Current reports, ledgers, code paths, and command outputs were not rerun here. |
| `theseus_operator_os` | Command vocabulary, durable work board, node registry, background/watch/wake contracts, skill registry, tool hooks, feedback routing, safety gates, TTLs, kill switches, signed updates, and isolation. | No Hive board, database, node registry, command channel, dashboard, or hook ledger was run. |
| `theseus_architecture_gate` | Pre-training checks for ratchet completeness, router readiness, safety ledger, regression suite, residual escrow, bridge benchmarks, procedural tools, routing memory, lifecycle governance, and external-inference zero. | Reported gate snapshot is not reproduced evidence; current gate artifacts were not regenerated or independently inspected here. |

## Mechanism

MoECOT is useful in the book only if it is treated as a runtime crosswalk, not a shortcut around evidence. Octopus Router and RMI define the modular route shape; Benchmaxxing defines the pressure and regression discipline; BeastBrain gives the local, stateful OS lineage; VIEA and Talos connect command contracts to execution artifacts; SCF constrains replacement; MoECOT and Project Theseus describe a compact orchestrator, routed specialist lanes, fail-closed ledgers, readiness gates, replay, handoff, and operator-visible control surfaces. The book's job is to map those runtime ideas onto the stack without pretending the runtime has been reproduced here.

The useful artifact is therefore a runtime evidence packet, not a name. A MoECOT run should be able to show the command it accepted, the route it chose, the specialist lanes it activated, the control-plane gates that passed or failed, the ledgers and replay references it emitted, the handoff state it produced, and the residuals that still block promotion.

A MoECOT Orchestration Record is the minimum public boundary for that mapping. It binds a run to its compact orchestrator, route head, specialist cores, control-plane gates, ledgers, replay references, handoff state, promotion blockers, residuals, and source-claim state.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Command or task"] --> B["Compact orchestrator"]
  B --> C["Route head"]
  C --> D["Specialist cores"]
  D --> E["Control-plane gates"]
  E --> F["Run, task, and replay ledgers"]
  F --> G["Handoff or delivery"]
  G --> H["Residuals and readiness updates"]
```

The runtime should not be one all-powerful agent. The compact orchestrator coordinates; the route head selects; specialist lanes do bounded work; control-plane gates fail closed; ledgers preserve evidence; replay tests the path; handoff makes ownership explicit; readiness records decide whether future routes should trust the same lane.

MoECOT also supplies a useful negative pattern for the book: an implementation-reference source must not be treated as proof merely because it is concrete. It can point to the records the book needs, identify missing artifacts, and motivate prototypes, while support states remain conservative until evidence is imported or reproduced.

## Interfaces

The interface is the MoECOT Orchestration Record.

Minimum fields:

- `run_id`
- `command_ref`
- `orchestrator_id`
- `route_head`
- `specialist_cores`
- `control_plane_gates`
- `ledger_refs`
- `readiness_gate_refs`
- `replay_refs`
- `handoff_refs`
- `promotion_blockers`
- `residuals`
- `source_claim_state`

Planning emits the command or task. Routing selects specialist cores. Execution runs typed jobs and adapters. Evidence and replay ledgers evaluate runtime changes. SCFs govern whether a runtime lane can become a default route or replacement implementation. Claim discipline decides whether a MoECOT statement is source-reported, locally reproduced, or still a design argument.

## Invariants

- MoECOT claims stay at `argument` unless artifacts justify promotion.
- Implementation evidence is separated from design argument.
- Runtime promotion follows readiness gates.
- Control-plane gates fail closed when evidence, authority, or replay is missing.
- A source-reported benchmark artifact is not a reproduced book result.

The key invariant is traceability across layers. A runtime route must be traceable to a command, specialist selection, authority envelope, readiness decision, ledger, replay status, and residual set. Without that trace, the runtime is not serving the stack; it is bypassing it.

## Failure modes

- Overclaiming unavailable source content.
- Treating implementation branding as architecture proof.
- Losing cross-layer traceability.
- Specialist lanes mutating authority without control-plane approval.
- Replaying happy paths while residual paths are ignored.

Overclaiming is contained by claim labels and support states. Branding is contained by schemas, fixtures, and reproducible commands. Traceability loss is contained by orchestration records. Authority mutation is contained by control-plane gates and SCF replacement transactions. Replay bias is contained by residual escrow and regression suites.

## Minimal implementation

The smallest useful implementation is an orchestration schema and fixture that records the runtime crosswalk without pretending a runtime was executed.

The `moecot_orchestration_record` schema is intentionally conservative. It records run identity, command reference, orchestrator, route head, cores, gates, ledgers, readiness gates, replay, handoff, blockers, residuals, and source-claim state. It gives future MoECOT work a place to attach real artifacts without promoting them prematurely.

The next evidence step is not more prose about MoECOT. It is importing or generating public-safe run, task, control-plane, replay, benchmark, and readiness artifacts, then recording whether each is source-reported, locally reproduced, or blocked. Until that packet exists, the chapter remains an implementation-reference map.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| MoECOT orchestration fixture validation | Check that the orchestration fixture matches the public schema and declares run, command, orchestrator, route head, specialist cores, gates, ledgers, readiness refs, replay refs, handoff refs, blockers, residuals, and source-claim state. | implemented by protocol validation; run in this pass |
| MoECOT source-ingestion gate | Check that MoECOT-specific claims cite inspected source notes or imported artifacts before promotion. | planned; not run |
| Runtime crosswalk completeness test | Check that a runtime record links command, route, cores, gates, ledgers, replay, handoff, and residuals. | planned; not run |
| Readiness/replay mapping review | Check that promotion decisions reference readiness and replay artifacts, not source branding alone. | planned; not run |

Only fixture-shape validation is implemented. Runtime claims require imported MoECOT artifacts, replay records, benchmark records, or reproduced local runs.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:moecot.runtime.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.MoECOTRuntime` | A runtime core promotion requires readiness, regression, and replay evidence references. | implemented |
| `lean:moecot.runtime.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.MoECOTRuntime` | A runtime claim sourced only from unavailable text cannot be promoted above argument state. | implemented |

These Lean hooks are implemented as finite-record predicates over runtime-core promotion reviews and runtime-claim source reviews. They prove only the record-level gates: runtime core promotion requires readiness, regression, and replay evidence references, and claims sourced only from unavailable text cannot promote above `argument`. They do not prove MoECOT runtime behavior, replay correctness, benchmark results, specialist-core quality, deployment readiness, or source-reported performance.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `octopus_router` | Octopus Router Architecture | routing_modular_intelligence | Lightweight head/router with dynamically loaded specialist arms and local boundaries. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `rmi` | Ratcheting Modular Intelligence | capability_ratchet | Benchmark pressure, residual escrow, verified modular capability, regression preservation. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `benchmaxxing` | Benchmaxxing: The Performance Ratchet | benchmarks_evidence | Benchmarks as pressure surfaces, saturation -> regression, harder frontier, anti-Goodhart safeguards. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `beastbrain` | BeastBrain Cognitive Architecture | whole_stack_lineage | Architecture lineage. Organism paradigm, SSD-native/geometrically verified intelligence. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `viea` | Verified Intent-to-Execution Architecture | whole_stack_execution_spine | Keystone source. Human intent -> command contracts -> artifacts -> routing -> runtime targets -> verification -> deployment -> feedback. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `scf` | Stable Capability Fields | governance_recursive_self_improvement | Use public release v1.0 when available. Stable boundaries, replacement, bounded authority, recoverable evolution. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `talos` | Talos Protocol | labor_execution_os | AI labor OS. Deterministic cognitive manufacturing, typed jobs, control planes, auditability, tool isolation. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `moecot_md` | moecot_agent_whitepaper.md | implementation_reference | Markdown export/source version. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `moecot` | MoECOT-Agent Architecture Whitepaper | implementation_reference | Concrete implementation evidence: governed low-parameter multi-core runtime, readiness gates, ledgers, replay. | source note available; connector or recovery required |
| `project_theseus_whitepaper` | Project Theseus Whitepaper | report_first_rmi_prototype | Local-first report-driven RMI implementation reference: SymLiquid, SparkStream, Octopus Router, residual escrow, self-evolution gates, Hive runtime, observability. | source note available |
| `theseus_operator_os` | Hive Operator OS and Work Board | labor_os_operator_surface | Shared command vocabulary, durable SQLite work board, node registry, background/watch/wake contracts, skill registry, tool hooks, feedback routing, and safety-visible operator surface. | source note available |
| `theseus_architecture_gate` | Theseus Architecture Gate | readiness_gate_governance | Pre-training readiness gate covering ratchet completeness, router readiness, safety ledger, residual escrow, bridge benchmarks, procedural tools, routing memory, lifecycle governance, and external-inference zero. | source note available |

The crosswalk identifies implementation-reference and routing/readiness sources. It does not treat MoECOT branding, source-reported benchmarks, or stale gate snapshots as reproduced evidence.

## Summary

MoECOT gives the book a runtime reference without letting runtime specificity dilute evidence discipline. It shows how a compact orchestrator, route head, specialist cores, fail-closed gates, ledgers, replay, and handoff can fit the stack.

The chapter's job is to make that implementation lane useful and honest: concrete enough to guide prototypes, bounded enough to avoid source overclaiming, and structured enough for future artifacts to promote claims only when they earn it. With routing and readiness bounded, the book can turn to compression and representation: how capability becomes cheaper, denser, and more reusable without hiding residual burden.

That transition matters because compression only helps the stack when the runtime can still say what was routed, what was verified, what became residual, and what remained too weak for promotion.
